title: Epsom salt and juniper berry muscle soak
subtitle: A restorative bath blend for tired muscles
excerpt: A bath soak built on Epsom salts, sea salt, and bicarbonate of soda, scented with juniper berry and rosemary essential oils. Juniper berry has a fresh, resinous character; rosemary adds warmth. Use after exercise or at the end of a physically demanding day.
sourceNotes: Magnesium sulphate (Epsom salt) percutaneous absorption debate: recent literature review in Nutrients journal (2017, open access) found evidence equivocal but bath use well-established as a traditional practice. Juniper berry essential oil: no known dermal sensitisation at standard bath concentrations. Rosemary essential oil at bath dilutions: well within safety parameters per Tisserand and Young, Essential Oil Safety (2nd ed., 2014).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 batch (~300 g, sufficient for 2 to 3 baths)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Essential oil content stays at 1% of dry salt weight for bath use.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in a sealed jar; the salts are hygroscopic and will clump if exposed to humidity.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store in a sealed jar away from moisture. The salt itself keeps indefinitely; the essential oil fragrance fades after 12 months. If the blend clumps, break up with a spoon before use.

glossaryTerms.definition: For bath products, apply a small amount dissolved in water to the inside of the forearm and leave for 20 minutes before first use. Juniper berry essential oil is well-tolerated at bath concentrations but individual sensitivity varies.

content.text: This is a dry bath soak: coarse salts, baking mineral (bicarbonate of soda), and essential oils combined in a jar and scooped into a running bath. The three salt components each contribute something different. Epsom salts (magnesium sulphate) dissolve quickly and are the main component by weight; there is ongoing debate about how much magnesium the skin actually absorbs, but the warm bath itself is relaxing regardless. Sea salt contributes a range of trace minerals and a slightly coarser texture. Bicarbonate of soda softens the water slightly and helps the salts dissolve more evenly. Juniper berry essential oil has a fresh, resinous quality, clean without being sharp; rosemary adds warmth and a herbal note associated with circulation. At 1% of the dry salt weight, the essential oil content is well within the range considered safe for bath use.

items.prepNote: 27% of formula; coarse or medium grain
0.7% of formula (approximately 40 drops); fresh, resinous note
0.3% of formula (approximately 20 drops); warming herbal note

content.text: Combine the Epsom salts, sea salt, and bicarbonate of soda in a mixing bowl. Drizzle both essential oils over the dry salts and stir well to distribute evenly. Transfer to the sealed glass jar immediately; the salts begin absorbing humidity from the air once the jar is open. Use within 1 year.
For a first use, complete a
: dissolve a small pinch in warm water and apply to the inside of the forearm for 20 minutes.

Add 100 to 150 g of the soak (roughly 4 heaped tablespoons) to a warm running bath. Stir to dissolve before getting in. Soak for 20 minutes. Rinse with clean water after the bath if skin feels tight; some people find the salt residue drying.

Store in the sealed jar away from moisture. Salt keeps indefinitely. The essential oil scent fades after 12 months but the soak remains functional. If the blend clumps with moisture, break up with a dry spoon before measuring.
